title: Outdoor pig water supply in winter
subtitle: Keeping troughs from freezing and water flowing to pigs in cold weather.
excerpt: Outdoor pigs in winter have the same water requirements as pigs in summer, a fully grown pig drinks up to 15 litres a day. Frozen troughs are a welfare breach that stops quickly. A few practical measures, trough positioning, insulation, and a twice-daily routine, keep the water flowing through a UK winter.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. AHDB Better Returns Programme pig welfare guide. Soil Association outdoor pig production guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A nipple drinker or open trough providing water to pigs on demand or at scheduled intervals.

content.text: that freezes overnight is a welfare breach. UK law requires that pigs have permanent access to adequate fresh drinking water. In sub-zero temperatures, an unmanaged trough stops being adequate by midnight. Daily checking at dusk, overnight insulation, and an early morning refill covers most UK winter nights.

A trough placed in direct contact with the ground loses heat quickly in hard frost. Raise it off the ground on timber blocks or set it inside a low shelter. The shelter does not need to be elaborate, a simple lean-to of corrugated metal over the trough traps enough radiant heat from the pigs' body warmth to delay freezing by several hours.

If you use an automatic ball-cock trough, insulate the supply pipe from the ground to the trough entry point. Water supply pipes freeze before the trough itself in most cases. Pipe lagging foam or armaflex tape on exposed pipes down to below the frost line is the practical fix.

For a small outdoor pig enterprise without electricity: use two troughs. Fill both at dusk with enough water for the overnight period. The pigs will drink one down by morning; even a trough with some ice on top still has liquid water underneath if it was full at nightfall.

In the morning, break any ice on the overnight trough, refill, and allow the other trough to freeze during the day as a backup. This simple rotation ensures water availability without any powered equipment.

A trough with an immersion heater element controlled by a frost thermostat is the most reliable solution where electricity is available. Set the thermostat to activate at 2°C. The element uses very little power and keeps the water just above freezing without heating it.

Pigs also get some water from their feed, particularly wet feeding. A pig receiving a wet feed ration in winter is less likely to suffer from frozen-water shortfalls than one on dry pellets. Even so, water access cannot be substituted by feed moisture alone, the legal requirement is clean fresh water, freely available.

Pigs will eat snow if nothing else is available. This is not an adequate substitute for water, eating snow reduces core temperature and increases energy expenditure to maintain body heat. Snow in the paddock is not a reason to skip checking the water supply.
